text: We are pleased to present you this video vignette of injection of mesenchymal stem cells, DARVAT, throstle, into perineal fistula of Crohn's disease performed by our team in Saint-Joseph Hospital in Paris. We present you the case of a twenty-year-old woman with Crohn's disease complicated by a high trans-sphincteric anal fistula since two thousand and fourteen. She had previously been treated with biologics and already had two sphincter sparing techniques. However, her anoperianal fistula was still evolutive. Anoperineal MRI confirmed the absence of residual suppuration and colonoscopy confirmed the absence of active luminal disease. She also underwent an exploratory procedure under anesthesia that confirmed good drainage and allowed performing cortage. Hence, we decided to treat this fistula with an injection of mesenchymal stem cells.

The MRI images display a high posterior trans-sphincteric anal fistula, indicated by a white arrow, filled with granulation tissue and lacking a liquid component. Additionally, there is pre-coccygeal focal inflammation without purulent collection, marked by a yellow arrow.
The patient is positioned in the lithotomy position in preparation for the procedure, which will be performed under general or spinal anesthesia.

The surgeon disinfects and cleans the perianal area using soap and water. The fistula with a posterior internal opening and high trans-sphincteric tract is visible. Curettage is performed using endoscopic brushes to remove granulomas and clean the fistula tract, while a seton remains in place to maintain visibility of the internal opening.

The surgeon closes the internal opening of the fistula using a circular needle with absorbable synthetic monofilament sutures. Three interrupted sutures are made to achieve a hermetic seal, and the satin is removed just before tying the knots.

The surgeon prepares four vials containing thirty million mesenchymal stem cells each, carefully reconstituting the solution with gentle movements to avoid cell damage. The cells are divided into four screw-type syringes of ten milliliters using an eighteen gauge needle, ensuring slow extraction to prevent cell lysis.

The surgeon performs a slow injection into the internal opening using a long, 21-gauge green needle. Multiple injection points are used to prevent loss of the product due to increased pressure. Half of the dose is injected into the internal opening and the other half into the fistula tract, with administration starting from the external opening and directed towards the internal opening. The injection is made approximately 2-3 millimeters deep into the walls of the fistula.
The surgeon gently massages the area where the injection was administered, covering the external opening with a small adhesive bandage. No gauzes or hemostatic products are placed in the anal canal.
